calendar.matters:
Petitioners Notice Of Hearing Writ Of Mandate
Off Calendar Jul-29-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER COREY SMITH's Hearing Writ Of Mandate.

Corey Smith's petition for a writ of mandate is denied.

San Francisco produces a voter information pamphlet in which candidates can buy up to 200 words to state their qualifications. Incumbent supervisor Dean Preston's 2024 statement says in part: "Voted to approve 30,000 new homes, 86% affordable." Smith would have this court strike that phrase from Preston's statement as "false and misleading." (Memo. 5:2; Elec. Code 13313(b).) However, such political speech is at the heart of the First Amendment. (Buckley v. Valeo (1976) 424 U.S 1, 15.) Thus, Smith had to adduce "clear and convincing proof" for each of his three arguments. (Elec. Code 13313(b); see also Utility Consumers' Action Network v. Public Utilities Com. (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 688, 698 ("clear and convincing" means "clear, explicit and unequivocal, so clear as to leave no substantial doubt" [cleaned up].) He does not.

First, Smith says homeless housing in 8,250 hotel rooms Preston voted to approve during the Covid-19 pandemic was "not real homes." (Memo. 6:7.) But even by a Smith definition of "home" - "one's place of residence; domicile" (id. at 6:15) - many thousand San Franciscans reside in hotel rooms for months. This linguistic fencing is not clear and convincing proof of falsity. Smith also quibbles that the City did not eventually "secure" most of the hotel rooms, but Preston's statement that he "voted to approve" them is undisputed. (Id. at 7:4-6.)

Second, Smith concedes Preston "voted in 2020 to place Proposition K on the November 2020 ballot to authorize?up to 10,000 residential units of low-rent housing projects," but claims it is misleading to say Preston approved the new homes, as voters and planning regulators must also approve. (Memo. 7:18-8:8.) Approval of Prop K by Preston and his board colleagues was step one to the other approvals. With a 200-word limit, a qualifications statement is truncated; it is not a civics tutorial. Preston's statement is not misleading.

Third, Smith takes issue with the 30,000 number, because the City reported approving 14,398 "housing development projects" from 2020 to 2023. (Memo. 8:14-23.) As already addressed above, Preston's statement is not limited to such "projects" - and permissibly so.

The requests for judicial notice are granted.

Proposed order submitted.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
